summary: Jeff Atwood explores the often-overlooked corollary to Fitts' Law: while commonly-used UI elements should be large and easy to click, dangerous or irreversible actions should be intentionally difficult to trigger. He introduces Alan Cooper's 'ejector seat lever' metaphor to describe destructive actions that must never be accidentally activated. Using Gmail's closely-placed Archive and Report Spam buttons as a cautionary example, and WordPress's well-separated Update and Trash controls as a positive one, Atwood argues that thoughtful UI design requires considering both what users should easily click and what they should not.
key_points: Fitts' Law states larger, edge-positioned UI elements are easier to click, but designers rarely consider its inverse
Dangerous or irreversible actions ('ejector seat levers') should be small, distant from common actions, and hard to accidentally trigger
Gmail's interface places radically different actions (Archive vs Report Spam) dangerously close together with only 3 pixels separating them
WordPress demonstrates good practice by making the common Update button large and prominent while keeping Move to Trash small and distant
Undo functionality is valuable but preventing accidental clicks in the first place is superior design
money_quotes: If we should make UI elements we want users to click on large, and ideally place them at corners or edges for maximum clickability – what should we do with UI elements we don't want users to click on?
The one thing that must never happen is accidental deployment of the ejector seat.
I can think of a half-dozen applications I regularly use where the ejector seat button is inexplicably placed right next to the cabin lights button.
Every few days I accidentally click Report Spam when I really meant to click Archive.
Undo is powerful stuff, but wouldn't it be better still if I wasn't pulling the darn ejector seat lever all the time?
Uncommon or dangerous UI items should be difficult to click on!
key_insight: Good UI design isn't just about making desired actions easy—it's equally about making dangerous actions hard to trigger accidentally.
